author: @UrbanBard1
text:
"A Safety Tip."  LOL

It's stand UP WIND. That is, put the wind to your back, so  that the fumes are blowing away from you DOWN WIND. 

They got this concept from water. If you stand on the bank of a stream,  Up Stream is where the water is coming from. Down stream is where the water is going to. Standing up stream is that you watch the water moving away from you,

author: @neobliviscarisa650
text:
As a H&S professional this industry is misunderstood by those who attempt to manage it! Most of the legislation is self regulated, HSE do not ban things! I work in high risk, we manage not averse to risks! 

Secondly , human behaviour causes over80% of accidents, so we try to deal with the hazard at source as a result. And risk ass considers competence and experience as mitigating factors

Clinker story was a joke by a teacher, not a serious intervention!

HSE law is based on evidence not assumption!!!

And we are one of the safest workplace in the world on average! It works

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Ne obliviscaris A I understand your need to defend your industry and appreciate it - but only to a degree - the theory of something and the practice of something always differ - you are talking theory - in practice we all know HSE has gone too far - the BBC here in England runs a course on how to carry your coffee - are you seriously telling me that hasn't gone too far?

replies.author: @neobliviscarisa650
replies.text: +Robert Murray-Smith tea control comment - that is not managing H&s proportionately according to the HSE principles of control! That is poor mgt and lack of competence of the BBC. Nothing to do with the HSE. I work in all industry including the police and military. I can assure you, the majority of HSE guidance and acop are not theoretical. They are based on lessons learned and forward thinking. And they work.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Ne obliviscaris A Mate - that's not what I am saying - i can see your point and agree with it - but some folks - and some pretty big companies take it too far - that's what i am saying. It's the difference between theory and practice. I am sure this plagues you and makes your job harder

replies.author: @neobliviscarisa650
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith Ok, I fair enough :-)

Yes, I often have to design exercises to burst peoples' bubble regarding what I do. In some ways, I enjoy the moment when they finally get it! I think teaching some elements in school would help, as we all aspire to go to work someday.

The funny thing is, when you find an angle people can relate to (like linking to home life). or the infinite number of amusing clips of h&s related stuff, it lends itself well to creativity, debate, being productive and understanding human behaviour. Even improving lifestyles too... Sadly, there are plenty of miserable and insecure people who apologise when delivering a safety induction to visitors or newcomers... We prepared to read and discuss a boring employment contract, but care less about the safety policy that is designed to keep us well. Its a no brainer to me... I do dangerous sports and simply respect manage those risks, not avert it...

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Ne obliviscaris A That's often the trouble mate - people in the know - well they know what’s going on and it's about correctly assessing the risk and taking appropriate steps to mitigate the risk - it's going too far and having no idea that annoys me - i have no problem with a sensible balanced approach - but in this i so often get an unbalanced approach lol - still i appreciate the input from a professional - cheers mate

author: @TimJW
text:
Good video. As far as I was aware H&S is about risk assessment, taking appropriate precaution to mitigate those risks, and personal responsibility - basically what you said at the end of this. It's not what you can and cannot do.

The problem is more down to litigation, ambulance chasing lawyers, and morons that want to sue for paper cuts or "I was given the wrong type of ladder" (that advert winds me up)!!!

text: I've worked with lead acid batteries a few times without gloves.  I've got acid on me a few times.  So what, I got a minor burn.   It's not life threatening.  I'm not swimming in acid.  The safety nuts need to relax.  I'm right there with you.  You're an expert and you know the risks.  If it's truly dangerous, you take the proper precautions.  Besides, anyone who tries to replicate your work has no business replicating it if they don't know the chemical dangers.

author: @wbeaty
text:
Don't forget, it's your channel, you make up the rules.  The real goal of safety-trolls is to get you arguing, so they attract attention to themselves.

For example, I post a "no trolls, zero tolerance" warning on all my videos.

Then, if any user is insulting others, I immediately remove their comment and block them permanently.  Do I go overboard?  Nah, youtube requires heavy-handed moderation.  I don't do this on any of my other forums, just youtube.  I've been in this biz for 25yrs now.

Perhaps consider adding something like "NO FAKE SAFETY WARNINGS, posting false accusations about danger will be dealt with harshly."  Put it on all your text captions.  Then be heavy handed when the obvious creeps ignore the warning.  But who decides what is a genuine legitimate safety issue?  You.  It's your channel.  If something actually presented a danger to children, then simply mark it 18+ so adult youtube users get access.   Perhaps a vid on making fireworks from kitchen chemicals would need the 18+.  That, and how to modify a fork so it actually fits into an electrical outlet!  :)   But working with open flame and battery acid?  That's no more dangerous than your typical cooking show.

Let the safety trolls go attack any cooks who dare to fry bacon without full protective garb and face shield.  Think of the children!  :)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I like what you say - A lot! - I think I am going to follow your example - thanks

replies.author: @ThePrimordialBeing
replies.text: If you don't argue, then you're expressing passive compliance.
